opp.next_step: The immediate next step is to schedule a detailed demo with Lester, Tim, Alechia , focused specifically on Discovery’s RFP workflow, evaluation scoring, functional assessments, supplier onboarding, Sage integration, and Merlin Intake through Teams. Before the demo, Zycus should send focused discovery questions around Sage data ingestion, supplier master data quality, approval workflows, current Onetrust pain points, and RFP weighting requirements. The demo should clearly show how Zycus can replace manual onboarding, automate RFP execution, support phased deployment to manage budget concerns, and deliver faster ROI through end-to-end S2P automation. Zycus should also plan the in-person visit during the first week of July to align stakeholders and build momentum.
opp.ais_why:
* Explicit customer excitement on the 26-May call: notes record "Excitement about agentic AI and guided intake for advanced procurement automation" (Lester Ash) and "Recognition of AI-driven data governance and ESG insights as critical capabilities" (Tim Damons).
* Positive reaction to Zycus AI specifically: "Merlin guided intake and agentic AI highlighted versus legacy systems; prospect reacted positively," with interest in a Merlin/Teams chat interface for self-service procurement requests.
* Tempered by readiness risk, not AI doubt: their "data is a mess," and past tool adoption stalled on budget; next steps are a detailed demo and a July in-person visit - real momentum but no POC/budget yet, hence Medium confidence.

metrics.narrative: Discovery has articulated the operational pain—manual P2P processes via the relevant stakeholder, non-automated and non-user-friendly supplier onboarding via Onetrust, no automation for RFP execution or evaluation scoring, messy procurement data impacting visibility and governance—but has not quantified the financial or business impact. Lester Ash described the pain on 2026-05-26: 'It's mainly manual. We use Sage as our financial tool, and for sourcing, pretty much manually. We've got Onetrust that we use as an onboarding tool for new service providers, but it's not very automated, it's not very user friendly, it's still manual.' Tim Damons added context on ESG and data governance becoming critical but unmet: 'ESG is becoming a very important aspect of procurement, understanding exactly what you're buying, when you're buying it, are you buying it,' and acknowledged procurement's immaturity: 'Discovery, even though it's been around for 32 years, it's still quite young when we talk about procurement in terms of its maturity model.' The Customer_Business_Problem__c field echoes this: 'procurement is still heavily manual across P2P, supplier onboarding, and RFP execution, creating bottlenecks in sourcing, approvals, supplier onboarding, data management, and evaluation scoring, while messy procurement data makes visibility and governance harder.' However, no quantified impact was stated—no cycle time, cost, headcount, error rate, or ROI target. The next move is to quantify the pain during the demo prep: ask Discovery to share current RFP throughput per month, supplier onboarding cycle time, manual effort (FTE hours), and rework/audit-trail incompleteness rate, then build a phased ROI case anchored to those baselines.

champion.narrative: Lester Ash (CPO, Decision Maker) and Tim Damons (Senior Procurement Manager, Evaluator) are engaged and knowledgeable but have not demonstrated champion behaviors (internal selling, executive introductions, driving urgency, pushing back on competitors for us, sharing internal docs, advocating for budget). Lester has direct access to the Decision Maker role (he IS the Decision Maker within procurement per contact role), but budget approval sits above him with the CFO or finance leadership, and he has not introduced Zycus to that Economic Buyer. Tim has good tactical access and context (he 'reports to Lester' per the call and 'leads the team that looks after IT, professional services, marketing'), but no advocacy actions are visible. Both participated actively in the 2026-05-26 discovery call, articulated pain clearly, and reacted positively to the relevant stakeholder and Merlin positioning per Avoma notes ('Merlin guided intake and agentic AI highlighted versus legacy systems; prospect reacted positively'), but engagement has been passive since then: 40 days of silence, no logged follow-up, and Lester's posture is exploratory ('not actively shopping, just understanding what's out there'), not urgent. Historical context tempers champion strength: Lester stated Discovery paused Zycus evaluations twice before (7 years ago and 3-4 years ago) because budgets were 'way out of appetite,' and after the second pause they 'invested in infrastructure' to support a local vendor instead, indicating Lester can champion alternatives or delay when budget is not aligned. The current state is 'developing': Lester and Tim are open, engaged, and interested in AI-driven automation, but they have not taken ownership of moving the deal forward internally, mapping the Economic Buyer, or creating urgency. The risk is that without a champion actively selling the business case to finance and driving the process, the deal stalls in exploratory mode and budget objections re-emerge. The next move is to develop Lester or Tim into an active champion by (1) re-engaging immediately to restart momentum, (2) co-creating a phased ROI case they can take to finance, (3) requesting an introduction to the Economic Buyer and positioning Lester/Tim as the internal sponsors who will drive approval, and (4) using the in-person visit (first week July, now overdue) to align stakeholders, secure executive sponsorship, and formalize the evaluation timeline.

competition.narrative: Discovery is evaluating Zycus against the status quo (manual P2P via the relevant stakeholder, maintaining Onetrust for supplier onboarding, no automation) and historical alternatives (Ivalua, Jaggaer, Coupa, a local South African vendor). The primary competitor is Do Nothing / maintain manual processes, not an active vendor evaluation. Lester Ash stated on 2026-05-26: 'not actively shopping, just understanding what's out there,' and described the current state: 'It's mainly manual. We use Sage as our financial tool, and for sourcing, pretty much manually. We've got Onetrust that we use as an onboarding tool for new service providers, but it's not very automated, it's not very user friendly, it's still manual.' Tim Damons referenced past RFPs: 'done a number of RFPs over the years looking at all the different tools in the market, whether that's Ivalua or Jaggaer or Coupa,' and Avoma notes captured 'Ivalua, Jaggaer, and Coupa were evaluated in past RFPs alongside Zycus,' but no current active evaluation against these vendors has been mentioned. The historical loss to a local vendor is instructive: Lester stated 'one of the companies that pitched was a local company which we wanted to support, so we invested in infrastructure to see if we can help them to develop into a competitive company in the local market that looks at private sector companies,' then 'unfortunately, that didn't work out, and a year and a half or two later we're back at square one.' The SF Competitors__c field is null, and How_are_you_addressing_your_problem_toda__c confirms 'broader P2P workflow is still largely manual, with procurement intake, sourcing, approvals, supplier onboarding, and RFP evaluation not yet fully automated.' The wedge is AI-driven automation (Merlin Intake, agentic AI, data governance, ESG insights) + comprehensive S2P suite + phased deployment to manage budget concerns, versus status quo pain (manual effort, poor user experience, no ESG visibility, messy data) or piecemeal tools that do not address end-to-end workflow. How we win: position Zycus as the practical path to procurement maturity (vs status quo immaturity), demonstrate phased ROI that pre-empts budget objections (vs prior 'way out of appetite' perception), and show Merlin/AI as the differentiator that legacy vendors and local tools cannot match. The risk is that without urgency and a quantified business case, Discovery defaults to status quo (Do Nothing) for another year.

identify_pain.narrative: The pain is confirmed and multi-dimensional: operational inefficiency from manual P2P processes, poor user experience and non-automation in supplier onboarding, lack of automation for RFP execution and evaluation scoring, messy and unstructured procurement data impairing visibility and governance, and inability to meet emerging ESG and data governance requirements. The pain owner is Lester Ash (CPO) and Tim Damons (Senior Procurement Manager), both of whom articulated it directly on the 2026-05-26 discovery call. Lester stated: 'It's mainly manual. We use Sage as our financial tool, and for sourcing, pretty much manually. We've got Onetrust that we use as an onboarding tool for new service providers, but it's not very automated, it's not very user friendly, it's still manual. It's just a way of sending information securely.' Tim added: 'ESG is becoming a very important aspect of procurement, understanding exactly what you're buying, when you're buying it, are you buying it,' and acknowledged Discovery's procurement immaturity: 'Discovery, even though it's been around for 32 years, it's still quite young when we talk about procurement in terms of its maturity model.' Lester also stated 'our data is a mess at the moment,' surfacing data quality as a barrier to automation effectiveness. The SF Customer_Business_Problem__c field synthesizes the pain: 'procurement is still heavily manual across P2P, supplier onboarding, and RFP execution, with Sage and Onetrust not providing the level of automation, usability, or orchestration they need, creating bottlenecks in sourcing, approvals, supplier onboarding, data management, and evaluation scoring, while messy procurement data makes visibility and governance harder.' The pain is confirmed, not inferred. However, the pain is RECOGNIZED but not URGENT: Lester stated 'not actively shopping, just understanding what's out there,' indicating the pain exists but Discovery is not in crisis mode. The risk is that without a compelling event or quantified impact, the pain may not be severe enough to overcome historical budget objections. The next move is to make the pain URGENT by quantifying it (current cycle time, manual effort, error rate, ESG exposure) and anchoring the demo to how Zycus eliminates the pain in Phase 1 (RFP workflow, Sage integration, supplier onboarding) with measurable ROI, then positioning Phase 2 (Teams intake, broader automation) as the path to procurement maturity.

decision_criteria.narrative: Discovery has articulated use-case and capability requirements but has not formalized evaluation criteria, scoring model, or weighted decision framework. The discovery call on 2026-05-26 surfaced explicit needs: (1) full RFP process with evaluation scoring, functional assessments, and weightings to confirm end-to-end workflow with no external workarounds (stated by Alechia Grobler per Avoma notes and inferred from Business_Objectives__c); (2) Sage ERP integration for initial data ingestion and ongoing synchronization (Lester Ash); (3) automated, user-friendly supplier onboarding to replace manual Onetrust workflows (Lester Ash); (4) Merlin guided intake via Teams interface for self-service procurement requests (positive reaction per Avoma notes and AIS_Why__c); (5) data cleansing and taxonomy management to address 'messy' procurement data (Lester Ash: 'our data is a mess at the moment'). These are capability asks, not weighted criteria. The SF Business_Requirements__c and Top_Challenges_Priorities__c fields are null, and no RFP scoring model or approval-chain criteria have been shared. Tim Damons referenced past RFPs ('done a number of RFPs over the years looking at all the different tools in the market, whether that's Ivalua or Jaggaer or Coupa'), indicating Discovery has run structured evaluations before, but the criteria for THIS cycle are not yet documented. The Gaps_identified_during_sales_demo__c field suggests Zycus planned to 'agree Phase 1 success metrics (choose 2-3): Intake-to-RFP kickoff time, Supplier onboarding cycle time, RFP throughput per month, Audit trail completeness / rework reduction,' indicating intent to co-create measurable criteria, but this has not yet occurred. The next move is to send the pre-demo discovery questions (per Next_Step__c) covering 'current RFP scoring model + approval chain' and 'Phase 1 success metrics,' then co-create weighted criteria during the demo that align to Discovery's pain (manual effort reduction, data governance, ESG visibility, faster onboarding) and can be measured in a phased deployment.

The absence is normal for a Qualified-stage deal where commercials have not started, but it becomes a blocker if not mapped before pricing and negotiation. The next move is to surface the paper process during re-engagement and the in-person visit: ask who handles legal review and contracting for procurement tools, whether InfoSec and ERP integration reviews are required, what the typical contracting timeline is, and whether a phased MSA/SOW (Phase 1: RFP workflow + Sage integration + supplier onboarding; Phase 2: Teams intake + broader automation) would accelerate sign-off and manage budget approval risk.

customer_expectations_fit.summary: Discovery's expectations are aligned with Zycus capabilities across RFP workflow automation, Sage ERP integration, supplier onboarding, Merlin Intake via Teams, and data governance/ESG insights. Positive reaction to AI positioning. No exposed gaps. Risk is budget perception ('way out of appetite' historically) and implementation complexity concerns ('data is a mess,' 'reliance on existing manual workflows'). Phased deployment and ROI quantification are critical to address these.

hard.ais_why:
* Explicit customer excitement on the 26-May call: notes record "Excitement about agentic AI and guided intake for advanced procurement automation" (Lester Ash) and "Recognition of AI-driven data governance and ESG insights as critical capabilities" (Tim Damons).
* Positive reaction to Zycus AI specifically: "Merlin guided intake and agentic AI highlighted versus legacy systems; prospect reacted positively," with interest in a Merlin/Teams chat interface for self-service procurement requests.
* Tempered by readiness risk, not AI doubt: their "data is a mess," and past tool adoption stalled on budget; next steps are a detailed demo and a July in-person visit - real momentum but no POC/budget yet, hence Medium confidence.
hard.next_step:
The immediate next step is to schedule a detailed demo with Lester, Tim, Alechia , focused specifically on Discovery’s RFP workflow, evaluation scoring, functional assessments, supplier onboarding, Sage integration, and Merlin Intake through Teams.

Before the demo, Zycus should send focused discovery questions around Sage data ingestion, supplier master data quality, approval workflows, current Onetrust pain points, and RFP weighting requirements.

The demo should clearly show how Zycus can replace manual onboarding, automate RFP execution, support phased deployment to manage budget concerns, and deliver faster ROI through end-to-end S2P automation.

Zycus should also plan the in-person visit during the first week of July to align stakeholders and build momentum.
hard.primary_competitor: Do Nothing / Manual P2P via Sage ERP
